Vers. 1. and when the people complain , it displease the lord . ] the word in the original ( here translate complain ) may also be render as it be in the margin , be as it be complainer , and so may intimate that they do only secret begin to murmur and mutter , and not break forth into such a open complaint as afterward they do at kibroth Hattaavah , when they lust for meat . therefore the lord hear this and be wroth ; so a fire be kindle against Jacob , and anger also come up against Israel . but in that psalm it be evident that the several passage of their murmur be not relate historical in order , but many thing be promiscuous insert here and there . however evident it be that the murmur for flesh , mention for . 4. be another from this , because it be say here that they weep again , and beside that be do at kibroth Hattaavah , this at Taberah . the cause of their present complain indeed be not express , but in all likelihood we may conceive it be because they be weary of follow the ark three day journey through the wilderness together without intermission , whereof there be mention make in the latter end of the former chapter for . 33. and the fire of the lord burn among they . ] that be , a fire send from God : but whether it break out of the earth , or from the pillar of fire , or be pour down upon they from heaven , it be not express . and thus their fiery tongue be punish with fire . and consume they that be in the uttermost part of the camp . ] this be ad , to let we see how the whole army escape when God send a fire among they , it be because the fire break out only upon the uttermost part of the camp ; and withal it may imply the cause of the punishment , because by these word it seem probable that in the uttermost part of the camp the sin begin , among those that be faint and wea•y with travel , as in Deut. 25. 18. how he meet thou by the way , and smite the hindmost of thou even all that be feeble behind thou , when thou be faint and weary : and he fear not God ; and upon they therefore the judgement do principal fall . and when you have serve your generation here , he receive you unto himself in glory . this be , and shall be the prayer , of Kings-Norton : Novemb. 3. 1659. your much oblige kinsman Tho. hall . to the worshipful , and his honour friend Richard GREVIS of Moseley hall , in the county of Worcester , Esq. the translator humble wish the multiplication of grace , and the continuation of peace . worthy sir : i have embolden my self to offer that unto the world , under your patronage , which , i believe , your singular modesty will almost decline , and fear , your excellent ingenuity will be ready to disow , when you find it so unworthy of the benign influence of your eye , much more of the effluence of your name , virtue , and authority , by which you be able to patronize any thing that make you its sanctuary of refuge . neither sir , have principle of policy only persuade i to inscribe your famous name , and entitle your worthy self to this my imperfection : but indeed , a desire to express and testify the esteem i have of the one , and the love and honour which i deserve have for the other . if any man , in this nice age , may be judge worthy to be the object of a dedication , it must be a Theophilus ; and , if any other , i do very believe , you be a Theophilus ; if a sober , moderate , contemplative , serious life , adorn with many public and private exercise of religion , expression of a gracious soul ; if constant and vigorous endeavour to reconcile difference amicable , and suppress sin authoritative ; if a engage , edify , aw presence , to wife , child , servant ; if a sweet , friendly , charitable own of God people ; or a cordial , gladsome entertainment of his faithful minister ; if any of these , if all these will denominate a man a lover of God , accept , i pray you , sir , this poor oblation ; and although i do not , can judge this little Impolite treatise worthy your countenance or patronage , yet i beseech you , for the love of God , to stir up all your whole soul ◊ improve your utmost interest to maintain and defend the cause which therein i study to patronize , which be not yet so much my cause , as the cause of all the faithful minister of Christ , and not so much they neither , as Christ . go on • beseech you , and , whatever trouble shall be upon the earth , whatever division , distraction , dissettlement , apostasy be , or may be in England , yet study to approve your self to be a friend to God cause , who you love ; and faithful to the interest of Jesus Christ , in who you believe . and so do , that you may be bless with day as prosperous as many , with a crown as glorious as eternal , be , and shall be the hearty prayer of sir , your friend and servant in Christ Jesus , Sam Shaw. Long-Whatton . July 2. 1659. to the impartial reader . reader ;

THomas de Vio Cajetanus , a italian and a cardinal . a man very well read in divinity , have study it even from a child , who for the happiness of his wit , and for his laborious diligence , become the prime divine of that and many more age , unto who there be no prelate or person in the council who will not yield in learning , or think himself too good to learn of he . this cardinal , go legate to Germany in the year 1523. study exact how those that err may be reduce to the church , and the arch-heretics convince , find out the true remedy , which be , the literal meaning of the text of the scripture in the original tongue in which it be write , and all the residue of his life , which be 11 year , he give himself to the study of the scripture , expound not the latin translation , but the hebrew root of the old , and the greek of the new testament . in which tongue , have no knowledge himself , he employ man of understanding , who make construction of the text unto he word by word , as his work upon the holy text do show . that good cardinal be wont to say , that to understand the latin text , be not to understand the infallible word of God , but the word of the translator , subject and obnoxious unto error . the discipline of the essenes . where enter into a strict confederation , or consociation , to lead together a collegiate devout life , they 1. shun all carnal pleasure , which may entice they from their devotion . 2. they avoid all profane company , and conformity to the world , as also all affectation of secular dignity , applause and honour . 2. they engage in a strict fraternal communion among themselves , profess a community of good , etc 4. they do in time of persecution , so far as they be able , lead a monastic life , forbear marriage , etc 5. they be very abstemious and moderate in the use of creature comfort ; forbear wine , drink water , etc 6. they have their distinctive garment , or white vestment . 7. they use ceremonial purification , accord to their law ; as also moral mortification of sin . 8. they enjoin silence on their novice , and be all studious for the right government of their speech , etc 9 they forbid oath . 10. they have their elder in great esteem . 11. they acknowledge all thing to be dispose by a particular over-ruling providence . 12. they do in a peculiar manner devote themselves to the worship of God by prayer , and sacrifice , especial off manimate . 13. they divide their live , and study into two part , 1. contemplative . 2. active : they spend their time most in action , beside what they employ in their devotion ; the principal study they addict themselves unto be medicine : they give themselves also to gardening , and other labour of the hand . 14. they distribute the day into time for prayer , for read , for study , for labour with their hand , and for natural refreshment . 15. they endeavour much exactness in their moral , to lead a exemplary life . 16. such as prove apostate , or scandalous , they excommunicate by the common consent of all the fraternity , or society . and to conclude with the character of Viret . ( in his interim page . 122. ) in sum their estate be in their first constitution a excellent school of medicine , of doctrine , and of example of virtue : all thing be do among they in good order , and i think the first christian Monks take their pattern from these essenes . but the late monk have rather follow the example of the sadduce , and Pharises . thus Viret .
